title: Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL / Bitly)
prompt: Design a service that turns long URLs into short codes and redirects users, handling ~100M new links and heavy redirect (read) traffic per day.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (URL shortener: "Google, Amazon, Meta")
Educative — System design questions asked by top companies

keyIdea: Read-heavy mapping problem: generate a unique short key, store key→URL in a horizontally scalable store, and serve redirects from cache.

notes.kv-store: Optimal. The core is a simple key→long-URL lookup at huge scale — exactly what a key-value store is built for.
notes.cache: Optimal. Redirects are extremely read-heavy with a hot tail; caching popular codes slashes DB load and latency.
notes.id-generator: Optimal. You need unique, collision-free short codes; a distributed ID generator (then Base62-encode) avoids coordination.
notes.load-balancer: Optimal. Redirect traffic is massive and stateless — spread it across many app servers.
notes.cdn: Acceptable. Helps geographically for the redirect hop, though the payload is tiny; secondary to caching.
notes.consistent-hashing: Acceptable. Useful once the KV/cache layer is sharded across many nodes.
notes.sql-db: Acceptable but not ideal. Works at small scale, but vertical limits make NoSQL the better fit at 100M/day.
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. A new short link being visible a few ms late is harmless; paying the latency cost of strong consistency is wasteful.
notes.geospatial-index: Poor fit. There is no spatial/location dimension to this problem.
notes.search-index: Poor fit. You look up by exact key, not by full-text search.

title: Design a News Feed (Instagram / Facebook feed)
prompt: Design the home feed: users follow others, and each user sees a ranked, recent timeline of posts. Handle the celebrity (large-follower) problem and fast feed reads.

sources.label: IGotAnOffer — Meta system design interview ("Design Instagram" is the most common)
DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked

keyIdea: Pick a feed-delivery strategy (fan-out on write vs read, hybrid for celebrities), precompute timelines in a fast store, and serve media from object storage + CDN.

notes.fanout: Optimal. The central design decision is fan-out-on-write vs on-read; the celebrity problem is the classic follow-up.
notes.cache: Optimal. Precomputed timelines and hot posts live in cache for fast feed reads.
notes.kv-store: Optimal. Per-user timelines and the follower graph scale best in a wide-column/KV store.
notes.object-storage: Optimal. Photos/videos are large immutable blobs — store them in S3-style object storage, not the DB.
notes.cdn: Acceptable. Delivers the media referenced by the feed to global users.
notes.message-queue: Acceptable. Fan-out work is done asynchronously off the write path.
notes.eventual-consistency: Acceptable. A post appearing a moment late in some feeds is fine; favors availability.
notes.load-balancer: Acceptable. Standard for the stateless read tier.
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. Feeds tolerate slight staleness; strong consistency adds cost for no user benefit.
notes.idempotency-key: Poor fit. This isn't a money/transaction path that needs at-most-once semantics.

title: Design a Distributed Rate Limiter
prompt: Design an API rate limiter (e.g. 'N requests per user per minute') that works correctly across a fleet of servers without letting users exceed their quota.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked ("Amazon, Google, Uber, Stripe, Microsoft, Atlassian, Pinterest")
LeetCode discuss — Uber | Rate Limiter (reported)

keyIdea: Keep per-client counters (token bucket / sliding window) in a shared fast store so all servers see the same count; enforce at the edge.

notes.rate-limiter: Optimal. This is literally the component being designed — token bucket or sliding-window log.
notes.cache: Optimal. Counters need a shared, atomic, low-latency store (Redis with INCR/TTL) visible to every server.
notes.api-gateway: Optimal. The natural enforcement point — reject over-limit requests before they hit services.
notes.consistent-hashing: Acceptable. Routes a given client's counter to a consistent shard of the counter store.
notes.tsdb: Acceptable. Useful for observing/alerting on request rates, not for the enforcement path itself.
notes.load-balancer: Acceptable. Present in any multi-server setup but not specific to rate limiting.
notes.sql-db: Poor fit. A relational DB per request is far too slow for counter increments at the edge.
notes.object-storage: Poor fit. Blob storage has nothing to do with fast counters.
notes.geospatial-index: Poor fit. No location dimension here.

title: Design a Chat System (Messenger / WhatsApp)
prompt: Design 1:1 and group messaging: real-time delivery, online/offline presence, delivery/read receipts, and message history.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (chat: "Meta, Google, and multiple FAANG")
Educative — System design questions asked by top companies

keyIdea: Hold persistent connections for instant push, route messages through a queue/fan-out, and store history + presence in a fast write-heavy store.

notes.websocket: Optimal. Real-time bidirectional delivery and presence need long-lived connections, not polling.
notes.message-queue: Optimal. Decouples senders from receivers and buffers messages for offline users.
notes.kv-store: Optimal. Message history and per-conversation data are write-heavy and key-addressed — ideal for Cassandra/KV.
notes.pub-sub: Acceptable. Good for fanning a group message to many recipient connection servers.
notes.cache: Acceptable. Presence and recent messages benefit from an in-memory layer.
notes.object-storage: Acceptable. For media attachments (images, voice notes).
notes.load-balancer: Acceptable, with the caveat that connections are sticky/stateful.
notes.sql-db: Poor fit at scale. Message volume is enormous and append-heavy; relational storage becomes a bottleneck.
notes.olap: Poor fit. Analytics store, not a real-time messaging path.

title: Design a Ride-Sharing Backend (Uber / Lyft)
prompt: Design driver–rider matching: track driver locations in real time, find nearby drivers for a request, dispatch, and compute ETAs/surge under strict latency.

sources.label: System Design Handbook — Uber system design interview guide
IGotAnOffer — Amazon system design (reported: "design Uber for a robo-taxi service")

keyIdea: Index driver locations spatially (geohash/quadtree) for nearby lookups, stream live positions over persistent connections, and keep matching low-latency.

notes.geospatial-index: Optimal. 'Find nearby drivers' is a proximity query — geohash/quadtree is the heart of the design.
notes.websocket: Optimal. Drivers stream location and riders get live updates over long-lived connections.
notes.cache: Optimal. Hot driver-location data must be read/updated in-memory at high frequency.
notes.load-balancer: Optimal. High-volume stateless request tier for matching/booking.
notes.message-queue: Acceptable. Dispatch events, trip lifecycle, and notifications flow asynchronously.
notes.tsdb: Acceptable. Surge pricing reads demand/supply signals over time windows.
notes.sql-db: Acceptable. The actual trip/payment records want transactional storage even if location data does not.
notes.search-index: Poor fit. This is spatial proximity, not full-text search.
notes.object-storage: Poor fit for the core loop (only relevant for documents/photos).

title: Design a Notification Service
prompt: Design a system that sends push, SMS, and email at scale (e.g. 10M push, 5M email, 1M SMS/day), with user channel preferences, retries on provider failure, and no duplicate sends.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (notifications: "Google, Amazon, Airbnb, Pinterest, Uber, Instacart")
Double Pointer — Notification service system design interview

keyIdea: Queue notifications, fan out to channel workers that call third-party providers, retry with backoff + circuit breakers, and dedupe with idempotency keys.

notes.message-queue: Optimal. Decouples producers from per-channel senders and absorbs huge spikes.
notes.circuit-breaker: Optimal. External providers (APNs, Twilio, SendGrid) fail; you must stop hammering them and fail over.
notes.idempotency-key: Optimal. Retries must not send the same notification twice — dedupe each send.
notes.kv-store: Optimal. Store user preferences, device tokens, and dedupe state with fast key access.
notes.pub-sub: Acceptable. Good for fanning one event to multiple channel consumers.
notes.rate-limiter: Acceptable. Respect per-provider and per-user send limits.
notes.cache: Acceptable. Cache user preferences/tokens on the hot path.
notes.load-balancer: Acceptable. Standard for the ingestion API tier.
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. Delivery is best-effort/eventual; strong consistency isn't the constraint.

title: Design Typeahead / Autocomplete
prompt: Design search-box autocomplete: as the user types each character, return the top-N most relevant suggestions in a few milliseconds, ranked by popularity.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (typeahead: "Google, Meta, and Netflix")
Double Pointer — Autocomplete/Type-ahead system design interview

keyIdea: Precompute a trie/prefix index of popular queries, serve top-N from an in-memory layer per keystroke, and rebuild rankings offline from query logs.

notes.cache: Optimal. Every keystroke is a lookup; suggestions must come from RAM (often a trie in Redis) for low latency.
notes.search-index: Optimal. A prefix/inverted index over the query corpus produces ranked completions.
notes.load-balancer: Optimal. Each keystroke is a request — high QPS stateless tier.
notes.olap: Acceptable. Offline aggregation of query logs computes popularity rankings.
notes.message-queue: Acceptable. Streams raw queries into the aggregation pipeline.
notes.cdn: Acceptable. Can cache very common prefixes at the edge.
notes.kv-store: Acceptable. Stores the precomputed prefix→suggestions maps.
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. Suggestions being slightly out of date is fine; latency matters more.

title: Design a Video Streaming Service (YouTube / Netflix)
prompt: Design upload, transcoding, storage, and global low-buffering playback of video, plus view counts and recommendations at massive read scale.

sources.label: Educative — System design questions ("Design a video streaming service like YouTube or Netflix")
DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked

keyIdea: Store originals in object storage, transcode asynchronously into multiple bitrates, and serve segments from a CDN; metadata/counts in fast stores.

notes.cdn: Optimal. Global low-buffering playback is impossible without edge caching of video segments.
notes.object-storage: Optimal. Raw uploads and transcoded renditions are huge blobs best kept in S3-style storage.
notes.message-queue: Optimal. Transcoding is slow, spiky background work — pipeline it through a queue.
notes.cache: Optimal. Video metadata, thumbnails, and view counts are extremely read-heavy.
notes.olap: Acceptable. Watch-history analytics and recommendation features run on a warehouse.
notes.eventual-consistency: Acceptable. View counts can lag slightly.
notes.kv-store: Acceptable. Video metadata and user state.
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. Playback/counts don't need linearizability; it would hurt scale.
notes.geospatial-index: Poor fit. No proximity query (CDN handles geography).

title: Design a File Storage & Sync Service (Dropbox / Google Drive)
prompt: Design upload, download, and multi-device sync of files: handle large files, deduplication, conflict resolution, and sharing.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (Drive/Dropbox: "Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, and Dropbox")
IGotAnOffer — Microsoft interview (reported: "design Dropbox/iCloud")

keyIdea: Split files into content-addressed chunks in object storage (dedupe), track chunks/versions in a metadata DB, and sync changes to devices via a queue.

notes.object-storage: Optimal. File chunks/blocks live in durable, cheap object storage.
notes.sql-db: Optimal here (as the metadata DB). File/version/chunk metadata and sharing ACLs want transactional, queryable storage.
notes.message-queue: Optimal. Sync notifications and chunk processing are asynchronous.
notes.cdn: Optimal. Download/sharing of files benefits from edge delivery.
notes.bloom-filter: Acceptable. Cheap pre-check for 'do we already have this chunk?' before dedupe lookup.
notes.cdc: Acceptable. Propagate metadata changes to sync/search subsystems.
notes.tsdb: Poor fit. Not a metrics problem.

title: Design a Payment System (Stripe-style)
prompt: Design charge processing: accept a payment, talk to external processors/banks, guarantee exactly-once charging under retries, and keep an auditable ledger.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (payments: "Stripe, Amazon, PayPal, and Capital One")
Educative — System design questions ("Design a payment gateway like Stripe")

keyIdea: Money demands correctness: idempotency keys for exactly-once charging, ACID/strongly-consistent ledger, and resilient async calls to external processors.

notes.idempotency-key: Optimal. The defining requirement — a retried charge must never double-charge the customer.
notes.strong-consistency: Optimal. Balances and ledger entries must be linearizable; no stale or conflicting reads.
notes.sql-db: Optimal. A transactional, auditable ledger is the textbook job for a relational DB.
notes.message-queue: Optimal. Calls to banks/processors and webhooks are async with retries.
notes.circuit-breaker: Acceptable. External processors fail; trip the breaker rather than cascade.
notes.wal: Acceptable. Append-only ledger / event sourcing for auditability.
notes.cdc: Acceptable. Stream ledger changes to reporting/fraud systems.
notes.eventual-consistency: Poor fit. Money cannot be eventually consistent — this is the trap answer.
notes.cache: Poor fit for the ledger path. Caching balances risks serving stale money data.

title: Design a Distributed Cache
prompt: Design an in-memory cache cluster (Memcached/Redis-style): distribute keys across nodes, handle node add/remove gracefully, set eviction/TTL, and stay available.

sources.label: IGotAnOffer — Microsoft/Amazon system design (reported: "design a distributed cache system")
DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked

keyIdea: Spread keys with consistent hashing so scaling moves few keys, replicate hot shards for availability, and define an eviction policy (LRU/TTL).

notes.consistent-hashing: Optimal. The key design problem: distribute keys so adding/removing a node reshuffles minimal data.
notes.cache: Optimal. You are literally building the in-memory cache layer.
notes.replication: Optimal. Replicate shards so a node loss doesn't drop a slice of the keyspace.
notes.bloom-filter: Acceptable. Avoid lookups for keys that definitely aren't cached.
notes.eventual-consistency: Acceptable. Replicas may briefly diverge; usually fine for a cache.
notes.load-balancer: Acceptable. Client routing, though consistent hashing often handles placement.
notes.sql-db: Poor fit. The whole point is to avoid the slow durable store on reads.
notes.olap: Poor fit. Analytics store, not a low-latency cache.

title: Design a Web Crawler
prompt: Design a crawler that fetches billions of pages: discover URLs, avoid re-crawling duplicates, respect politeness/robots, and scale across many workers.

sources.label: DesignGurus — FAANG questions, ranked (web crawler across major prep platforms)
Educative — System design questions asked by top companies

keyIdea: A URL frontier (queue) feeds workers; a Bloom filter / seen-set dedupes URLs; fetched pages go to object storage; politeness throttles per-domain rate.

notes.message-queue: Optimal. The URL frontier is a giant queue feeding fetcher workers.
notes.bloom-filter: Optimal. Cheaply answer 'have we already seen this URL?' across billions without huge memory.
notes.object-storage: Optimal. Store the raw fetched HTML/pages as blobs.
notes.rate-limiter: Optimal. Politeness — cap requests per domain so you don't hammer sites or get banned.
notes.kv-store: Acceptable. URL metadata, crawl timestamps, and dedupe backing store.
notes.consistent-hashing: Acceptable. Partition the frontier/seen-set across workers.
notes.search-index: Acceptable. The downstream indexing step (if asked to make pages searchable).
notes.strong-consistency: Poor fit. Crawling tolerates duplicates/staleness; coordination overhead isn't worth it.
